text: These are cheap, poorly made masks that don't fit snugly against the face and consequently shouldn't be worn as protection against Covid. Don't waste your money and endanger your life wearing these. Buy N95 or KN95 masks instead.
It's a lot larger then I thought it was going to be, I hope it will work.

PRO
- cheapest CO2 sensor i could find
- seems to take somewhat reliable CO2 readings (400 outside, 700 in a normal room, 1000-1500 in a bedroom after sleeping with closed door and windows)
- small and lightweight

CON
- CO2 graph doesnt seem to be accurate (it shows 1335 all time max, but ive seen the "current" reading at 1500+ within the log period)
- very confusing menu
- doesnt have a battery compartment (it has a micro USB port for power, and you can use a smartphone battery pack, but why isnt there a built in battery or at least a battery compartment?)
- readings update very slowly (it takes 10+ minutes to get accurate readings after moving it to another room)
- temperature and humidity readings dont match my other sensors, both are slightly higher than they should be
These are ok for hobby use

So far so good. My original Prusa textured sheet with the batch number TL-21 is a pile of garbage, nothing stuck to it no matter what I did.
I simply pulled this out of the package, wiped with 99% IPA and printed using Inland silk rainbow PLA.
The. Print. Just. Stuck. Just like the prusa textured sheet was "supposed to". Now I can actually use my printer. I'll put this sheet through its paces in the coming days to see how it holds up. I attempted to print the 3D Hubs Marvin in the picture numerous times on the factory sheet, it always failed and popped off mid print. With this sheet it printed the 1st time without any intervention. I'm happy with my purchase at this point.

I bought this as a spare for when my slowly dying original unit finally bit the dust. The heater and thermistor wires were a bit out of place so I had to unplug and rearrange them. After that all went smoothly for a month until the extruder just started clicking and not turning. I took the unit apart to diagnose and everything was smooth as butter. Turns out I damaged the ribbon cable at some point. As soon as I replaced that, this unit has been working flawlessly.

This thing is amazing with a couple of caveats.
Woks and water running are very audible even from across the room even when behind equipment however I was able to find the leak eventually in between.

The sensitivity/ volume control seems a little sketchy when on the lower end but with a little experience I was able to zero it in.
Found a very small leak the first time I used it.
It will hear refrigerant flowing through pipes and that might continue while the system equalizes.
It tried to fool me until I realized what I was hearing.
It zeros in much closer than a gas leak detector.
It's definitely a worthwhile tool that I will use often.

Really, this shouldn't be advertised for glasses at all.

It DOES NOT REMOVE OIL. Smudges, fingerprints, oil, will stay.

That's all I wanted it for - fast and hands free. (I then realized even if it worked, you still have to dry the water off..so there wouldn’t be any savings).

It just removes hidden grime. The water got clouded from it after cleaning 1, and especially 3 of my glasses. But on all 3, smudges remained on the lenses. Bummer.

Also, the scratches warning is DEFINITELY a problem. What glasses have no even teeny scratches?

I didn't have a catastrophe, but did have a tiny scratch, and with wishful thinking I did it anyway, 90 secs, and indeed, the scratch when from a smooth, thin line, to being chunkier - twice as wide..making the scratch 50% worse in a sense. I'd definitely NOT use it even one more time. That’s a problem.

So I returned it.

BUT, I'd guess it works GREAT on all other things advertised since it clearly removed grime that I didn't know was there. And it IS simple, fast, and easy.

UPDATE2:
great customer support!
they sent me a new firmware, and a replacement awair glow device to make sure the device itself isnt the problem.
the device itself worked the same as my original awair glow, but the new firmware they pushed to my device works much better (version 1.2.3 versus the original 1.0.0)
all readings are MUCH more accurate now with the new firmware!

temperature and humidity readings are still slightly too high (~1C and 8%), and the co2 readings get more and more inaccurate the higher the CO2 concentration is in the room (150ppm too high @~1000ppm) , but overall its more or less accurate now. good enough to get an idea of the air quality.
its still not as good as my other cheaper temperature and humidity and CO2 sensor, but this one has an app and allows me to track and log and access everything via my phone.
i hope they can improve sensors even more with better firmware updates in the future.
right now, in my opinion, the temperature and humidity readings are not accurate enough to use them to control a fan or dehumidifier via the built in power plug relay switch... but fingers crossed, hope they can improve this further.
if they do, ill post another update.

UPDATE:
it keeps disconnecting from wifi
the app lost all readings and doesnt display anything anymore
i wasted so much time already trying to just make it work... but even when it works, for a few hours, the readings are all over the place and useless

AIR QUALITY READINGS
temp readings are accurate
humidity displayed as 10-20% too high
VOC readings are very inaccurate
CO2 readings are very inaccurate and report CO2 as going DOWN from 1700 to 1500 when it actually went UP from 600 to 900 so this seems to be completely broken
the app is buggy and often doesnt update the readings
the app drains my battery and uses a ton of resources (android)
it forces you to turn on GPS and share your location with the app, which im not happy about either

i live in a hot and humid environment so i have a couple of sensors already, some very basic and some very expensive
ones, including an accurate PM2.5, PM10, VOC and formaldehyde sensor and a professional CO2 sensor.
i had high hopes for awair glow, being able to track it all and log it on my phone... sadly the readings are very inaccurate, and there is no way to fine tune or calibrate anything.

SMARTPLUG
the smartplug functionality works, i can remotely turn devices ON and OFF
there is a delay of 2 seconds, but thats acceptable in my opinion

NIGHTLIGHT
the nightlight works, but the motion sensor is extremely weak and only detects motion within a 30cm radius, which isnt really helpful. it essentially turns on when you almost bumped into it. the light it emits is sufficient, but the glowing ring on top of the awair glow is directly pointed at your eyes, and in a pitch black room its way too dark and literally burns into your retina and hurts to look at. i dont know why the LEDs are even pointed upwards... most night lights focus on illuminating the FLOOR and cast indirect light to the floor to be easy on the eyes.

So far I used this to clean the tub and mildew in corners near the ceiling of bathroom. It took about the same amount of time as, but much much easier than scrubbing by hand. Like all such devices, this handy device measures the conductivity of water, which, at a given temperature, depends in a nearly linear fashion the concentration of dissolved salts in the water. The conductivity (which is one of the measurements it will display) is converted into parts-per-million (PPM) of dissolved solids (mostly salts) through a correction factor (which is usually in the 0.5-0.9 range). This device also measures temperature, presumably to use it as a corrective factor. It's easy to use -- just press a button on its face and stir the business end through the water for 5-10s, then read the value -- you can cycle through the values by pressing the "mode" key.
My reason for giving it three stars is that its conversion factor from electrical conductivity to total dissolved solids seems to be very small -- less than 0.5. I use it for my saltwater pool: it shows 4950 for electrical conductivity, but gives me a clearly wrong 2330ppm for TDS. A chemical test for NaCl (one of several varieties of salt in the pool, but the most important for a saltwater pool) shows 3000ppm -- and the TDS includes the sodium chloride and so should be at least as high; indeed, another brand of TDS measuring pen gives a TDS of 3300ppm.
If, however, you consider only the electrical conductivity and use your own correction factor to derive the TDS, things look a lot better: with the most common correction factor (2/3), I would get 3300ppm for TDS, which is what that other brand gets.
So, good for measuring EC (conductivity) and temperature, but you need to derive the TDS yourself from the EC -- don't even bother reading the value provided by the device.

Model train hobby requires TINY nails, in fact MUCH smaller than these wire nails. They are sold as scale model rail spikes and they are expensive relative to the quantity you get. While these are too large for rail spikes generally, they are very useful for a number of other things including fence posts, mail boxes, flag poles etc. Hard to find a package of just small nails, they usually are marketed as assortments which leaves me way too many Picture hanging nails and not enough hobby sized. You can never have too many tiny nails and screws in model railroading.

I have experience with 3D printers, so I was able to overcome the obstacles that this kit provided. My main issue is with the hot-end. Put simply, it doesn't work well at all. Jamming is a very common occurrence with the stock hot-end. Yes, I assembled it correctly with the bowden tube fully against the heat-break. I replaced the hot-end with an E3D-V6 which required printing a new mount/effector because the supplied hot-end is a different shape. Once this was done, it worked much better. But I did a little more.

The Repetier firmware that this comes with is... not the greatest. It generally works, but all of the square-root calculations (a requirement of a delta printer) it's asking of the board is a lot, and makes for very jittery printing. I was going to put Marlin on it, but it requires the same sort of processing power. That's when I discovered Klipper. Klipper does require a Raspberry Pi which does all the heavy calculations, then sends the basic movement commands to the printer over USB. The printer runs buttery smooth now. Klipper was very easy to install and configure.

Presales at SIIG said that the unit would scale from a single source to accomodate a 2X2 video wall array of TV's. I could not get it to do that.  The documentation in the box says that it does not scale at all. It is also unclear if it will support anything but a horizontal row of displays.  The documentation indicates that for a 2X2 at 1024 X768 you need 2048 X 1536 and so on.  I tried to contact support a number of times and they did not answer or return messages left for them.  I was really looking forward to this working but it seems like they just don't have it properly described and are not focused on customer satisfaction.  I have returned it to Amazon for a credit and will buy a different soluiton.

The only issue I have with this thing is the fact that it only works after I bang it repeatedly. The reading will either be blank (as if the devise is dead) or it will show me readings that aren't even readings at all (there are no numbers, but instead little flashes of digital lines). So after smacking it on my knee over and over and over again, the number 0 will finally come up and I am able to dip it into the water and test it and get an actual reading.

Btw, my tester is not old. I've had it for less than a year and it has always been like this since day one. I'm not sure what the issue is exactly, but seeing as it's been this way since I received it in the mail I recommend a better tester.

After several failed prints I wrote a nasty review. After some more trial and error, I determined my bed adhesion issue was operator error. So here is the revised review.
The magnetic sticker went down smooth as glass with no bubbles at all. I cleaned the bed very well and followed the instructions provided with the QR code in the packaging. Take your time and go slow, your patience will be rewarded.
As for the bed adhesion it took a few attempts, but I determined my Z height needed to be a good bit lower than with my glass bed. In order for PLA to stick to the textured surface you really have to squish it into the tiny crevasses. Once I figured this out all my prints stick and get a lovely surface finish from the textured side. I didn't have to change any of my temp or 1st layer settings other than the correct Z height. So far I am pretty happy with it since I figured out what I was doing wrong.

Let me start by saying that I already have an Airwolf3d 5.5, which I bought completely assembled. I then bought an Airwolf3d 5.5 kit which I assembled myself.

I purchased this kit to build a Prusa i3 from scratch, and it works, but there are a lot of things I don't like about this kit:
The RAMPS board had a short across two pins on the back of the board. Thankfully, I noticed this before I plugged anything in and was able to clean it up with the touch of a soldering iron and a solder sucker.
The heatsinks are not attached to stepper motor drivers. You have to buy special glue to attach them. Without the heatsinks, the stepper drivers will overheat. Without the glue, the heatsinks move around and can easily short out the RAMPS.
Two of the stepper drivers were bad, but were replaced free of charge.
The kit has a separate SD card reader which is unnecessary because the LCD has one built in.
They claim that the extender board for the fans is "plug and play". But you have to solder pins onto the RAMPS because the aux 12v power does not have pins already soldered on. There is very little online support at geeetech regarding how to configure these fans. Turns out they are on pins 6 and 11, but figuring out the sw config was time consuming. They only work correctly in one very specific config that is contrary to the marlin settings. Incorrectly configured, it causes the X and Y travel to stall and jump.
The belt is not the standard belt for reprap machines. You need to tweak the X and Y motor step values to account for the different belt. Belt clamps also have to be modified to hold them correctly. The belt is rather ridged and causes some inconsistent movement.
There is no thermistor for the heat bed
The hotend is not assembled very well and plastic slowly leaks above the nozzle
I couldn't get the limit switches to work at all. These mechanical switches are board mounted, and I suspect the board mimics opto switches. I never figured them out so replaced them with standard micro switches.
The NEMA 17 motors were smaller than I expected and I wound up replacing the two for the Z axis with larger motors

I do like:
The price
The LCD with the built in SD card reader.
The hot end has two nozzles, .35 and .4 mm
The heat bed and borosilicate glass
Lots of wires to connect things

I did construct a fully functional 3d printer using most of the parts in this kit. But there is very little support or reference documentation to help you. The printer makes very accurate parts, within .2% tolerance.

If you are skilled in electromechanical device repair and maintenance, skilled in software configuration, and you have plenty of time to research and figure things out, you might have fun with this kit.

2014.10.01 UPDATE
After 7 months of use, the hot end resistor burned up. I would have expected a lot more use out of it. By Comparison, Airwolf hot ends in use for over a year are still going strong. Good news: a replacement [[ASIN:B00CHS0KCG 3D Printer Hot End Heater Resistor]] is only $8.

What isn't made clear anywhere on the manufacturer's Web site or here on Amazon is that this device is only compatible with Windows XP. If you do not have Windows XP you cannot access the logs, not even on the device itself. There is literally no way to view what has been logged without connecting to a Windows XP machine. If you're lucky enough to find a Windows XP machine that you can use to retrieve data, you quickly find out that the only way you can get the data is through the 30+ year old technology of hyperterminal. CSV, Excel, tab-delimited file? What's that? If you have hundreds of log records you need to parse, make a sandwich and get out the comfy chair because you're going to be doing a lot of cutting and pasting.

They have tick marks every 2 ml, which is inconvenient if my most common dosage is 5 ml. The tick marks they use are too wide to have them every 1ml. The tick mark style used in these syringes makes it harder to measure the liquid. ½ inch refers to the needle length. Most people are better off with a shorter needle since injections do not need to go deep. I use these for peptides. 30G is a bigger needle size than 31G. 30G is harder to jab into the skin and will leave a bigger hole. I prefer the 31G size. 30G will fill faster if you are mixing the peptides with BAC water, and it might be better if you are injecting a full 100 ml since it will be faster. Thinner needles are more prone to bend.

After having used these for a while I can conclude that it is significantly harder to pierce the skin than other brands of needles. The tips seem dull and I am having to use blunt force rather than piercing to get into the skin.

I prefer the EasyTouch syringes due to their thinner more accurate tick marks. The current price for these Vaxxen Labs syringes is $17 for 100, which is $0.17 each. EasyTouch costs $15 for 100, although prices vary.

I bought this robot vacuum based on reviews I read. It is the first robo vac I’ve ever bought so my review may be based on what I thought a robot vacuum should do. First off, it does not map to your floor plan. It says it doesn’t in the details but I didn’t know any better. It operates in a criss cross pattern, which may or may not get everywhere you’d like. We do not have any carpet and our floors are dark so everything shows up on them. We have two cats and it does a good job picking up hair but I don’t feel it really gets the larger bits as well. Because it operates in the crisis cross pattern it seem like it misses areas.

The app is just okay. I’m very tech savvy and I was able to easily program it but I can’t for the life of me figure out how to change or stop that program. My biggest beef with this vac is that it gets stuck ALL THE TIME. I’ve found myself not using it because it’s just too much hassle to keep picking it up and moving it every time it gets stuck. I have it programmed to start cleaning at 4am each day and there has not been a day yet that I haven’t come down in the morning to find it stuck. It gets stuck on doorways, under furniture, and especially on the bases of our bar stools.

After my experience with this product, at least I know what to look for in our next one.

When you drill into sheet rock, the anchors are a bit tight to the screw. Meaning, once you screw in and it catches, it doesn't always go all the way into the anchor-- instead the whole thing anchor and screw start engaging deeper into the wall. You have to plan for that and be careful. This is different than my experience with other anchors bought at our local hardware store.

Regular electrical tape is not good for small wires, it's too thick, too wide and it usually comes apart. I use this to fasten wires to model train parts and it has held up so far. To insulate wires it's easier to use liquid tape and just make sure the wires are not touching each other. Kapton tape makes that simple, one or two strips or wraps and the job looks neat. I have seen small fabric backed taped used in electronics but have been unable to find any.

This is a _nearly_ perfect replacement for my Vitamix 5200. There were two reasons that I took a star off (1/2 star each).

1) The shaft is not quite the same as the OEM shaft. This one is completely round, the OEM shaft is round with one flat side. Therefore my existing knob didn't fit. Not a biggie, I just had to get another knob. [[ASIN:B000HMC1XA Tops 55713 Fitz-All Replacement Appliance Knobs, Set of 2]]

2) The alignment tab was bigger than the OEM tab. I had to bend it in to get it to fit. Again, not a biggie, but it's not quite a perfect fit.

That said, the OEM pot was, quite frankly, garbage. No doubt the cheapest thing they could find (for a $500 appliance!!). This pot works perfectly; the speed control is as smooth as this appliance should have always been, which was never :(

For DIY'ers fixing a 5200, it's pretty easy: Pull off the rubber gasket (where the jar/blade assembly sits), and remove the 4 screws. Remove the drive spline using a 5/64th allen wrench (find the arrow and a tiny hole to insert the wrench into). Separate the two halves of the body. Cut the old wires off the old pot (as close to the pot as possible). Strip and solder the wires to the new pot and switch them out. Be careful to put the vibration dampeners back on the body if they came off. Reattach the drive spline and fit the rubber gasket back over the top. Done.
